title: Integral of sec(x)

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-03-calculus-linear-algebra.md

setup.narrative: Compute the definite integral of sec(x) from 0 to pi/6.
rules: Standard derivative facts: d/dx[sin x] = cos x, d/dx[cos x] = -sin x.
You may use any integration technique.
setup.question: What is the value of integral from 0 to pi/6 of sec(x) dx?

learningObjectives: Recognize when no obvious substitution or by-parts works.
Search for an antiderivative by computing derivatives of plausible candidates.
Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to evaluate at the bounds.

stages.label: Why naive techniques fail
stages.prompt: Try u-substitution with u = cos(x) on integral of 1/cos(x) dx. What happens?

options.text: Stuck -- du = -sin(x) dx but the integrand has no sin(x) factor to absorb. u-substitution doesn't apply.
Substitution works directly to give -ln|u| + C.

stages.explanation: u-substitution requires the derivative of u to appear in the integrand. Here d/dx[cos x] = -sin x, but the integrand 1/cos(x) has no sin(x). Stuck. Same problem with by-parts. We need to GUESS an antiderivative and verify.

stages.label: Choose your technique

options.text: Hypothesize: try ln|sec(x) + tan(x)| as an antiderivative. Differentiate and check.
Numerical integration -- the integral has no closed form.

stages.explanation: Recognize-by-reverse-differentiation. The expression sec(x) + tan(x) shows up because d/dx[sec x] = sec x * tan x and d/dx[tan x] = sec^2 x. Sum: d/dx[sec x + tan x] = sec x * (sec x + tan x). The factor (sec x + tan x) cancels when we take ln.

stages.label: Verify the antiderivative
stages.prompt: Compute d/dx[ln|sec(x) + tan(x)|]. What do you get?

options.text: [sec(x) * tan(x) + sec^2(x)] / [sec(x) + tan(x)] = sec(x) * [tan(x) + sec(x)] / [sec(x) + tan(x)] = sec(x).

stages.explanation: Chain rule: d/dx[ln u] = u'/u where u = sec(x) + tan(x). u' = sec(x) tan(x) + sec^2(x) = sec(x) * [tan(x) + sec(x)] = sec(x) * u. So u'/u = sec(x). The factor of u cancels -- giving us our antiderivative for free.

stages.label: Evaluate at the bounds
stages.explanation: cos(pi/6) = sqrt(3)/2 and sin(pi/6) = 1/2. So sec(pi/6) = 2/sqrt(3) and tan(pi/6) = 1/sqrt(3). Sum = 3/sqrt(3) = sqrt(3).

stages.label: Commit final answer
stages.prompt: Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
stages.explanation: FTC: integral from 0 to pi/6 of sec x dx = ln|sec(pi/6) + tan(pi/6)| - ln|sec(0) + tan(0)| = ln(sqrt(3)) - ln(1) = ln(sqrt(3)) = (1/2) ln(3) ≈ 0.549.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: There's an alternative trick: multiply numerator and denominator of sec(x) by (sec(x) + tan(x)) before integrating. Walk through that approach.
What's the integral of csc(x)? (Symmetric trick.)
Why doesn't the absolute value matter on (0, pi/6)? When does it matter?
